answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: "The three scenarios developed by the construction and property sector are: • An orderly 1.5°C scenario where decarbonisation policies are enacted immediately and smoothly • A disorderly scenario where significant decarbonisation is delayed until 2030, which leads to global warming being limited to <2°C by 2100 • A hot house scenario where global warming reaches >3°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100, due to no further decarbonisation policies being enacted and emissions continuing to rise"
answers.notes: The S&P Global Climanomics physical risk tool also uses SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5 as underlying pathways for the quantitative physical risk assessment, but the three named scenarios above are the primary strategic scenarios used by Stride.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: 1.5°C (Orderly scenario), below 2°C (Disorderly scenario), and greater than 3°C (Hot House World scenario), all relative to pre-industrial levels by 2100. No temperature overshoot is explicitly mentioned.
answers.evidence: "The world succeeds in limiting warming to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100 ... leading to limiting global warming to below 2°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100 ... No further effective climate policy is enacted; global emissions continue to grow, which leads to greater than 3°C of physical warming above pre-industrial levels by 2100"

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: The New Zealand Green Building Council (NZGBC), with Beca facilitating the development of the scenarios through workshops.
answers.evidence: "The sector scenario analysis for the construction and property sector was led by the New Zealand Green Building Council, with involvement from entities across the value chain within the sector. Beca facilitated the development of the scenarios, through workshops involving the technical working group."
answers.notes: The S&P Global Climanomics tool (used for physical risk assessment) uses SSP pathways, but the primary strategic scenario provider is the NZGBC.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: "The tool provides a risk assessment (expressed as a percentage of loss relative to total asset value) across different climate outcomes, based on the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways of SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5."
answers.notes: Only SSP identifiers are mentioned; no RCP identifiers are listed anywhere in the document. There is no mixing of SSP and RCP scenario families.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: "Stride has undertaken a review of the scenarios to test the applicability of the scenarios to Stride's business and to customise the scenarios as required to focus on those aspects that are likely to challenge the Stride business strategy. A management working group considered the scenarios and any specific changes required to reflect Stride's business... Stride considers that the construction and property sector scenarios, as customised by Stride and described in this report, are relevant and appropriate for assessing the resilience of Stride's business model and strategy to climate-related risks and opportunities"
answers.location_hint: Page 46, Applicability of scenarios to Stride section
answers.notes: The sector scenarios were developed for the NZ construction and property sector, and Stride further customised them for its own business context.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: "There were 45 organisations involved in the development of the sector scenarios, including listed property companies, retirement village and aged care operators, property developers and materials suppliers. A number of workshops were held over a period of approximately 9 months, with the draft scenarios being reviewed by the working group as well as interested stakeholders such as major investors." and "Stride was an active participant in the development of the sector scenarios for the construction and property sector, including being involved in both the leadership group and the technical working group."
answers.notes: While the exact keywords "participatory" or "co-created" are not used, the description clearly indicates a collaborative, multi-stakeholder workshop-based development process involving 45 organisations over 9 months, which constitutes co-produced scenario development.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: Yes — the S&P Global Climanomics tool uses a historical baseline for physical risk assessment. The document states the tool "reflects the climate-related change in the level of hazard exposure of an asset over time, relative to an historical baseline." However, no separate named baseline or reference scenario is described for the strategic scenario analysis (the three narrative scenarios).
answers.evidence: "The S&P Global Climanomics tool assesses risk associated with the physical effects of flooding (fluvial and pluvial), temperature extremes, tropical cyclones, drought and wildfire, and reflects the climate-related change in the level of hazard exposure of an asset over time, relative to an historical baseline."
answers.notes: A historical baseline is referenced only in the context of the S&P Global Climanomics physical risk tool. No explicit baseline/reference scenario is described for the three strategic narrative scenarios.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: "During FY24 Stride undertook an initial assessment of the potential physical impacts of climate change across all properties owned and managed by Stride using the S&P Global Climanomics software modelling tool, and also undertook an assessment of the risk of sea level rise using the NZSeaRise and NIWA Sea Level maps."
answers.notes: These tools are used for physical risk assessment. No IAMs or macroeconomic models are named for the strategic scenario narratives.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: "The S&P Global Climanomics tool assesses risk associated with the physical effects of flooding (fluvial and pluvial), temperature extremes, tropical cyclones, drought and wildfire... The tool provides a risk assessment (expressed as a percentage of loss relative to total asset value) across different climate outcomes." Also: "Physical risk: Increased frequency and severity of weather events results in higher costs through repairs to buildings, higher insurance costs and rates... An increase in frequency and severity of extreme weather events may lead to increased capital expenditure to retrofit buildings to improve their resilience to weather events, as well as increased operational costs from repairing damage."
answers.location_hint: Page 45 (physical risk assessment) and Page 53 (climate-related risks table)
answers.notes: The S&P Global Climanomics tool explicitly provides loss estimates as a percentage of asset value for acute physical risks including flooding, tropical cyclones, and wildfire. The risk register also discusses damage costs from extreme weather events.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: "New Zealand still faces moderately severe physical impacts of climate change with an increase in extreme wind speeds (up to +5%), rainfall intensity (+6%), and number of hot days (+40%) by 2050." / "The shadow carbon price rapidly increases after 2030 to reach $250/tCO2e by 2050" / "Population growth/urbanisation, Drought/heat/wind, Investor/tenant expectations, Electricity supply, Extreme weather events, Insurance costs, Land use changes, Managed retreat, Availability/cost of building materials, Property value changes, Government decarbonisation policies"
answers.notes: The driving forces diagram on page 47 explicitly lists these variables. Quantitative values are given for wind speed, rainfall intensity, hot days, and carbon price in the scenario narratives.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: "New Zealand still faces moderately severe physical impacts of climate change with an increase in extreme wind speeds (up to +5%), rainfall intensity (+6%), and number of hot days (+40%) by 2050." (New Zealand-specific localised data within global scenarios) and "The tool provides a risk assessment (expressed as a percentage of loss relative to total asset value) across different climate outcomes, based on the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways of SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5." (global SSP pathways used in S&P Climanomics tool)
answers.notes: The strategic narrative scenarios are global in framing but include New Zealand-specific (regional/local) physical impact data. The S&P Global Climanomics tool uses global SSP pathways but assesses individual property-level (local) risk. NZSeaRise and NIWA Sea Level maps are New Zealand-specific tools.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Sector scenario development uses: Short term: present–2030; Medium term: 2031–2050; Long term: 2050–2100. For Stride's own risk assessment: Short: present to 2030; Medium: 2030–2040; Long: 2040–2050. Rationale is provided for both sets of timeframes.
answers.evidence: "The time horizons considered in development of the scenarios are: Short term: present – 2030; Medium term: 2031 – 2050; Long term: 2050 -2100... longer term time horizons were used, out to 2100, as the physical impacts of climate change are most extreme at these longer timeframes." / "In considering climate-related risks, Stride has used the timeframes short (present to 2030), medium (2030 - 2040) and long (2040 - 2050). These were felt to be the most appropriate for Stride, given the lifecycle of buildings, and Stride's strategic planning horizons. Stride plans in 10 year cycles for capital and maintenance expenditure on the buildings it owns and manages."
answers.location_hint: Page 47 (scenario time horizons) and Page 62 (risk management time horizons)
answers.notes: Two different sets of time horizons are used: one for the sector scenario narratives (out to 2100) and a shorter set for Stride's own risk assessment (out to 2050). Both are explicitly justified.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: "These scenarios were selected as they were considered to provide the greatest test of the strategy and approach of the participants in the sector." / "Stride considers that the construction and property sector scenarios, as customised by Stride and described in this report, are relevant and appropriate for assessing the resilience of Stride's business model and strategy to climate-related risks and opportunities, as the scenarios consider the factors that are most relevant to Stride's business and have the most potential impact on shaping Stride's strategy and business model, as reflected in the driving forces."
answers.location_hint: Page 46, Scenario Analysis and Applicability of scenarios to Stride sections
answers.notes: Justification is provided both at the sector level (greatest test of strategy) and at the Stride-specific level (most relevant factors, most potential impact on business model).
